1: Ki yesh lakesef motsa’ (surely silver has its mine/source) — motsa’ = outgoing, source, point of exit — the vein of ore from which silver emerges. Vemaqom lazahav yazeqqu = “and a place for gold where they refine it” — zaqaq = to refine by filtering/straining (the pi’el intensive). The chapter opens with the premise that human technology can locate and extract every precious mineral from the earth — a premise that will be systematically applied and then overturned for the one substance (wisdom) that defeats human mining.
2: Barzel me’afar yuqqah (iron is taken from the earth/dust) — afar = dust/earth (the same word as Adam’s origin, Genesis 2:7). Ve’even yatsaq nehushah = “and from the ore-stone copper is smelted” — yatsaq = to pour/cast/smelt; nehushah = copper/bronze. The smelting of copper from ore requires the same principle: the precious metal is hidden in and extractable from the matrix of common stone.
3: Qets sam lahoshekh (he sets a limit/end to the darkness) — qets = end, limit, terminus; lahoshekh = the darkness of the mine shaft. The miner’s lamp penetrates what was dark, setting a boundary on darkness by pushing it back. Uvekhol-takhlit hu hhoqer = “and to every extremity he searches” — takhlit = the uttermost limit. ’Even ’ofel vetsalmavet = “the stone of gloom and deep shadow” — the deepest, darkest rock that no natural light reaches.
4: Parats nahal me’im-gar (far from where people live a shaft is opened/broken) — parats = to burst/break through; nahal = a wadi/stream-channel or, here, a mine shaft cut through the rock. Nishkahim min-ragel = “forgotten by the foot” — nishkah = forgotten (niphal of shakah). The mine shafts run directly beneath surfaces where people walk, completely unknown to those feet above. Dallum me’enosh nau’u = “far from mortals they dangle and sway” — the miners hanging in shafts far below, remote from human society.
5: ’Erets mimmenah yetse’-lahem (the earth — from it bread comes out) — the contrast: the surface produces agricultural food (lahem = bread/food); vetahteyha nehepakh ke’esh = “but underneath it is turned up as though by fire” — the violent transformation of rock by mining (or possibly volcanic imagery). The same earth that produces nourishing bread on its surface is being burned and turned upside down underneath.
6: Meqom-sappirim ’avaneha (its stones are the source of sapphires) — sappirim = sapphires/lapis lazuli (the dark blue stone associated with YHWH’s throne, Exodus 24:10; Ezekiel 1:26). Ve’afrot zahav lo = “and it contains flakes of gold” — afrot = dust/fine flakes; gold dust embedded in the rock. The earth contains all these treasures but keeps them hidden — requiring the labor of breaking in to find them.
7: Netiv lo-yeda’o ’ayit (a path no bird of prey knows) — ’ayit = a bird of prey (hawk, vulture; the term for a bird with the sharpest possible vision, used to emphasize the imperceptibility of the mine paths). Velo shezafattu ’ein ha-dayya = “nor has the eye of the falcon observed it” — dayya = a falcon/kite. Even the keenest vision in the natural world cannot discern the path to the mine.
8: Lo-hidrukhuhu benei-shahhats (the proud/fierce beasts have not walked it) — benei-shahhats = literally “sons of pride” — the powerful wild animals (lions). Velo ’avhar ’alav shahal = “nor has the fierce lion passed over it” — shahal = a young roaring lion. Even the most powerful predators do not know the path to the mining depths. The chapter constructs an argument by escalation: the birds with sharpest vision and the beasts with greatest power are equally ignorant of this path.
9: Baseles shalah yado (he puts his hand to the flint/hard rock) — seles = flint, the hardest rock (used for cutting implements in ancient technology). Hapakh mishores harim = “he overturns mountains from their roots/foundations” — hapakh = to overturn, turn upside down (a strong verb of radical inversion; used of Sodom’s overthrow, Genesis 19:25). Human technology is powerful enough to uproot mountains in the search for ore.
10: Bayurot ’avot yeqaq (he cuts channels through the rocks) — yeqaq = cuts/hews; channels (yurot) cut through solid rock to allow access. Vekhol-yaqar ra’atah ’eyno = “and everything precious his eye sees” — the miner’s eye systematically takes in every precious thing (yaqar = precious, rare, honored) that the opened rock reveals.
11: Mibeki neharot hibesh (he holds back the springs/streams from flowing) — beki = weeping/trickling (used for tears; here for trickling water in underground streams). Vatatsafun yotsi’ or = “and brings hidden things out to the light” — tsafun = what is hidden/buried (from tsafan = to hide/treasure). The miner controls water flow and brings what was hidden to light. This verse is the peak of human technological achievement in the hymn — the turning point before the “but where shall wisdom be found?” of v12.
12: Veha-hokhmah me’ayin timmatse’ (but wisdom — from where shall it be found?) — the rhetorical pivot of the entire chapter. me’ayin = from where? — literally “from what nothingness?” (’ayin = nothingness, the absence). Wisdom is being sought like a mine location, but no map or technology can locate it. Ve’ey zeh maqom binah = “and what is the place of understanding?” — maqom = place, site; binah = discernment/understanding (from bin = to distinguish, see between). If the chapter just proved that every precious mineral has a maqom (v1–11), wisdom has no maqom that human exploration can find.
13: Lo-yada’ ’enosh ’erkah (no mortal knows its worth/way to it) — ’enosh = frail mortal human (chosen over ’ish or adam to emphasize human limitation). ’erkah = its ordering/worth/path (from ’arak = to arrange, evaluate). The double meaning: no mortal knows wisdom’s value (cannot price it) and no mortal knows its arrangement (cannot find its location). Velo timmatse’ be’erets hahayyim = “and it is not found in the land of the living” — the land of the living (’erets hahayyim) is the domain where human searching operates; wisdom is not there.
14: Tehom ’amar lo-vi hi’ (the Deep says “it is not in me”) — tehom = the primordial cosmic deep (Genesis 1:2; Psalms 42:7; Job 104:6), personified as a cosmic voice in this passage. Veyam ’amar ’ein imadi = “and the Sea says ‘it is not with me’” — both the Deep and the Sea (yam) speak, searching their own depths and reporting wisdom’s absence. The cosmic realms that dwarf human experience are themselves ignorant of wisdom’s location.
15: Lo-yutan segor tahteyha (pure gold cannot be given/paid for it) — segor = closed/refined gold, a gold bar (from sagar = to close/shut). Velo-yishaqel kesep mehirah = “nor can silver be weighed out as its price” — shaqal = to weigh (same root as shekel, the standard weight-unit of silver). The chapter shifts from geography (where?) to commerce (what price?): wisdom cannot be located geographically or purchased commercially.
16: Lo-tesulleh bektem-’ofir (it cannot be matched/valued against the gold of Ophir) — sulleh (pu’al of salah) = to be weighed/valued. Ktem-’ofir = the gold of Ophir, the finest gold known in the ancient world (1 Kings 9:28; Psalms 45:9; Isaiah 13:12). Beshoham yaqar veesappir = “against the precious onyx and sapphire” — the most precious gems of the ancient Near East are insufficient as equivalent value for wisdom.
17: Lo-ya’arkennah zahav veukhukhit (gold and glass/crystal cannot equal its value) — ’arakh = to arrange, compare, be equal to (same root as ’erkah in v13). ukhukhit = glass/crystal (probably fine transparent glass, which was extremely valuable in the ancient world). Utemurat ah yeraqah fine gold = “its exchange cannot be in items of fine gold” — the fine gold jewelry is insufficient for the trade.
18: Ram vegalit lo yizkker (coral and crystal are not worth mentioning) — ram = coral (from the sea floor, expensive and exotic); galit = crystal or pearl (uncertain). Vemeshekh hokhmah mippeninim = “the acquisition of wisdom is above rubies” — meshekh = drawing/pulling out (the effort of obtaining something valuable); peninim = red coral, rubies, or pearls (cf. Proverbs 3:15; 31:10 where a capable wife is worth more than peninim). The comparison to Proverbs 3:15 and 31:10 places this wisdom hymn within the Solomonic-wisdom tradition.
19: Lo-yesawennah pitdat-kush (the topaz of Cush cannot compare with it) — pitdah = topaz (Exodus 28:17, in the high priest’s breastplate). Cush = Ethiopia/Nubia, the source of the finest topaz. Bektem tahor lo te’arakh = “against pure gold it cannot be priced” — the culmination of the commercial inventory. Every precious material that human commerce knows — gold (various grades), silver, sapphire, onyx, glass, coral, ruby, topaz — is explicitly declared insufficient to purchase wisdom.
20: Veha-hokhmah me’ayin tavo’ (where then does wisdom come from?) — the rhetorical question is repeated from v12 (nearly verbatim: timmatse’ = be found → tavo’ = come). The second question is slightly different: not “where is it found?” but “where does it come from?” The change of verb (ba’ = to come/arrive) shifts the focus from location to source. Ve’ey zeh maqom binah = the exact repetition of v12b, bracketing the commercial inventory as a literary bracket.
21: Venilmah me’einei kol-hay (it is hidden from the eyes of every living creature) — nilmah = it is concealed (niphal of ’alam = to hide, conceal; cf. Job 6:10 where Job says he has not concealed the Holy One’s words). Umippof hashsayim nisteirah = “and hidden from the bird of the heavens” — the birds of the sky (who had the keenest vision in v7) are equally in the dark. The living world, from earth-dwelling animals to the flying birds, is universally ignorant of wisdom’s location.
22: ’Abaddon vamavet ’ameru (Abaddon and Death say) — ’Abaddon = the place of destruction, the realm of the dead (from ’abad = to perish; appears only in Job, Proverbs, and Psalms in the OT; later used in Revelation 9:11 as the name of the angel of the abyss). The personification of both the cosmic deep (v14) and the realm of the dead speaks: even Sheol’s rumor-network (“we have heard a whisper”) only catches rumors of wisdom, not its location. The universal ignorance spans from the living world to the dead.
23: ’Elohim hevin darkah (God understands the way to it) — the resolution of the chapter’s search: only God (’elohim) has wisdom’s derek (way, path). Vehu yada’ et-meqomah = “and he knows its location” — maqom (place) finally finds its answer: God knows it. The mining metaphor is resolved: God is the only mining engineer who knows the vein location. He has been seeking wisdom in the wrong direction — it cannot be extracted by human technology; it can only be given by the one who knows its location.
24: Ki-hu liknot ha’arets yabbit (for he looks to the ends of the earth) — God’s cosmic vision spans the entire created order. Tahat kol-hashamayim yir’eh = “and sees everything under all of heaven” — the scope is total: every point under the sky is within God’s field of vision. Only such cosmic omnivision could know where wisdom is hidden when even the Deep, the Sea, and Abaddon report ignorance.
25: La’asot laruah mishqal (when he assigned a weight to the wind) — mishqal = weight (same root as shaqal = to weigh, and shekel). YHWH weighs the wind — a remarkable image of divine precision over the seemingly uncontrollable. Vemayim tikken bemiddah = “and measured the waters by measure” — tikken = he established/measured (from taqan = to set right, measure). God’s precision over wind and water contrasts with the ungovernable appearance of weather.
26: Beasoto lematar hoq (when he made a decree for the rain) — hoq = statute, decree, appointed limit (the word for laws inscribed in stone). YHWH gave the rain (matar) a decree that governs it, like a legal statute. Vedderekh lahaziz qolot = “and a path for the lightning and thunder” — the storms are not chaotic but follow derekh (a path, a way) that God has established. The same vocabulary used for wisdom (“where is wisdom’s derek?” v23) appears here: the storms have a derek because God gave it; wisdom has its derek for the same reason.
27: ’Az ra’ah vayesapperah (then he saw it and made it known/declared it) — saphar = to count/number/declare (root of sefer = book, and mispar = number). God sees wisdom and “tells” it — numbers it, declares it, records it. Hekhinah vegam-haqarah = “he established it and fully searched it out” — kun = to establish, make firm; haqar = to search/probe thoroughly (same word used of the friends who “searched” Job in Job 29:16). God has already done the full investigation of wisdom; it does not remain to be found by human searching because it has already been found, established, and declared by God.
28: Vayyomer le’adam (and he said to mankind) — this is the chapter’s resolution. After establishing that only God knows wisdom’s location, God communicates to ’adam (humanity, generic) the accessible form of wisdom. Hen yir’at ’adonay hi hokhmah = “look — the fear of the Lord is wisdom” — yir’ah = fear/reverence. The same four-descriptor of Job in 1:1 ended with yare’ ’elohim (fearing God); the wisdom hymn concludes that this very fear is the wisdom that is inaccessible to human mining but accessible through relationship with God. Vesur mera’ binah = “and to turn from evil is understanding” — the same phrase that described Job at the book’s opening (sar mera’, Job 1:1). The conclusion of the greatest wisdom poem in the OT is: Job already has wisdom; he is defined by the qualities the hymn names as wisdom.
